text: own way. She found small pleasures where she could. Where she couldn’t, she gritted her teeth and pressed on . A mile south of the human slums, Ronin stopped. Without meaning to, he’d followed the route Lara had taken the day he’d made his offer to her — down Morrie, into the old residential ruins of southern Cheyenne. He saw it as it was now — the crumbling remains of houses where humans and bots had once dwelled in peace — and how it had been that day. Relentless rain, small rivers flowing in the cracked streets, puddles filling the breaks in the concrete . His memory replayed Lara, defeated and soaked, as she knelt over the storm drain at his feet. Her tears were indistinguishable from the rainwater . Ronin tilted his head, staring down at the clogged drain. A flash of reflected light caught his attention. He dropped to one knee, enhancing his optics to locate the source. Centuries of buildup cluttered the drain; chunks of concrete, scraps of tattered cloth, and broken pieces of wood . And there, lodged amidst the refuse, was a bit of gold . Holding the strap of his rifle in place with his left hand, he reached into the rubble with his right. He clawed through the dirt and debris. The clutter shifted, collapsing atop itself, and a cloud of dust filled the air. Ronin withdrew his arm and opened his fingers . He held a ring on his open palm. A slender gold band, free of adornment save the single diamond set on its outside. After brushing dirt from it, he took it between forefinger and thumb and raised it to the light . This was what she’d found, that day. What she’d lost. The ring was precious metal, and would fetch a high price at the Market. For Lara to have been so close to pulling herself out of squalor, only to trip and accidentally throw her salvation away … How would it have felt, if he’d held the key to his core programming in his hand and lost it a moment later ? He tucked the ring into his inside coat pocket and rose, swinging his optics toward Cheyenne. What would Lara think if he went back now and gave her the ring ? No. There was work to be done, and three days was already too short a time. She’d be there when Ronin returned, no matter what terrible outcomes his simulations suggested as possibilities . Ronin pulled on his mask, goggles, and gloves, and raised his hood . It was only three days . * * * I n the early years after Ronin’s reactivation, many of the old signs were still intact. He’d committed so many of them to memory — Nebraska, Iowa, Kansas, Dakota, Sioux City, Omaha, Amarillo. Dozens, hundreds, thousands of places. Those names were lost to time, now; everything was dust . There’d been a sign here, too. Welcome to colorful Colorado , it said. Only a single wood post remained, rotted and torn apart by insects. The nearby building was a pile of old roofing and crumbled bricks. Husks of vehicles lay half-buried in the dirt all around, but there was little left of value, unless he decided to drag a gutted car frame back to Cheyenne . He continued south along the road, following the sparse patches where dust hadn’t been able to settle. Coarse, brown grass jutted from cracks in the ruined pavement, swaying in the breeze. Another landscape that was everchanging, but never truly any different . This was what the people of Cheyenne had faced. This was the landscape they thought they’d be forced into. Mile upon mile of dirt and shifting dunes, with only the toughest vegetation clinging to a meager existence. This was why they’d fought . There was no question — the bot leader mentioned in the journal was Warlord. Had he been justified in his actions? Had it been a matter of survival, of his people against the humans ? Or had it been genocide ? Ronin cut west, toward the dark mountain peaks on the horizon. There were no answers to those questions out here. He’d ended more than his share of people, often without remorse; he couldn’t pass judgment . Still, didn’t such destruction run counter to what the Creators stood for? The humans described in the journal — a broken, frightened people, clinging to the remnants of their old lives — couldn’t have posed much threat to Warlord and his bots . Part of Ronin — gaining strength with each step — wanted only to return to Lara. She wasn’t entirely safe in Cheyenne, not while Warlord and his gearheads ruled, but there was more to it . There was a chance he’d eventually discover the nature of his programming out in the Dust. But with Lara, he might learn how to live . He passed the ruins marking the edge of Fort Collins as the sun sank toward the western horizon. The green grass and living trees were naturally occurring here, if still somewhat sparse, and might have attracted more settlers if not for its location. This was the edge of the dust, no-man’s land, too harsh to offer true prosperity . The lingering effects of the long-ago cataclysm farther south didn’t help . They say Denver’s just a radioactive crater . He’d gone there once, more than a hundred years ago. The devastation stretched for miles — buildings flattened, an entire city wiped out of existence. His sensors had picked up trace levels of radiation, even keeping his distance. It was no place for man or bot . Here, fifty miles away, entire towns had been spared such destruction. Decades of disrepair and constant scavenging had taken a toll, but places like this still held treasures . And dangers .
